story: Lighting fixtures come in a wide variety of styles for various functions. The most important functions are as a holder for the light source, to provide directed light and to avoid visual glare. Some are very plain and functional, while some are pieces of art in themselves. Nearly any material can be used, so long as it can tolerate the excess heat and is in keeping with safety codes.
q1: Lighting fixtures can be made from nearly any material as long as it tolerates heat and meets safety codes. (true/false)
q2: The primary function of lighting fixtures is to serve as art pieces. (true/false)

story: In a movie theater, steps in the aisles are usually marked with a row of small lights for convenience and safety, when the film has started and the other lights are off. Traditionally made up of small low wattage, low voltage lamps in a track or translucent tube, these are rapidly being replaced with LED based versions.
q1: Movie theater aisle lights are being replaced with LED versions. (true/false)
q2: Movie theater aisle lights use high-voltage lamps. (true/false)

story: In some design instances, materials used on walls and furniture play a key role in the lighting effect< for example dark paint tends to absorb light, making the room appear smaller and more dim than it is, whereas light paint does the opposite. In addition to paint, reflective surfaces also have an effect on lighting design.
q1: Dark paint absorbs light and makes rooms appear smaller. (true/false)
q2: Reflective surfaces do not affect lighting design. (true/false)

story: Motion picture and television production use many of the same tools and methods of stage lighting. Especially in the early days of these industries, very high light levels were required and heat produced by lighting equipment presented substantial challenges. Modern cameras require less light, and modern light sources emit less heat.
q1: Motion pictures and television use the same tools and methods as stage lighting. (true/false)
q2: Modern light sources emit less heat than older sources. (true/false)

story: The small, head-mounted device measures an individual's daily rest and activity patterns, as well as exposure to short-wavelength light that stimulates the circadian system. The device measures activity and light together at regular time intervals and electronically stores and logs its operating temperature. The Daysimeter can gather data for up to 30 days for analysis.
q1: The Daysimeter can gather data for a maximum of 15 days. (true/false)
q2: The Daysimeter measures both activity and light exposure. (true/false)

story: Lighting control systems reduce energy usage and cost by helping to provide light only when and where it is needed. Lighting control systems typically incorporate the use of time schedules, occupancy control, and photocell control (i.e.daylight harvesting). Some systems also support demand response and will automatically dim or turn off lights to take advantage of utility incentives. Lighting control systems are sometimes incorporated into larger building automation systems.
q1: Lighting control systems cannot be incorporated into building automation. (true/false)
q2: Lighting control systems can automatically dim lights based on utility incentives. (true/false)

story: The Illuminating Engineering Society of North America (IESNA), in conjunction with organizations like ANSI and ASHRAE, publishes guidelines, standards, and handbooks that allow categorization of the illumination needs of different built environments. Manufacturers of lighting equipment publish photometric data for their products, which defines the distribution of light released by a specific luminaire. This data is typically expressed in standardized form defined by the IESNA.
q1: The IESNA publishes guidelines and standards for illumination. (true/false)
q2: The IESNA works independently without collaborating with other organizations. (true/false)

story: During the Age of Enlightenment, philosophers such as John Locke advocated the principle in their writings, whereas others, such as Thomas Hobbes, strongly opposed it. Montesquieu was one of the foremost supporters of separating the legislature, the executive, and the judiciary. His writings considerably influenced the opinions of the framers of the United States Constitution.
q1: Thomas Hobbes supported the separation of powers. (true/false)
q2: Montesquieu's writings influenced the framers of the U.S. Constitution. (true/false)

story: Congress has the sole power to legislate for the United States. Under the nondelegation doctrine, Congress may not delegate its lawmaking responsibilities to any other agency. In this vein, the Supreme Court held in the 1998 case Clinton v. City of New York that Congress could not delegate a "line-item veto" to the President, by powers vested in the government by the Constitution.
q1: Congress shares lawmaking authority with the President. (true/false)
q2: The nondelegation doctrine prohibits Congress from delegating lawmaking to other agencies. (true/false)

story: A common misperception is that the Supreme Court is the only court that may determine constitutionality; the power is exercised even by the inferior courts. But only Supreme Court decisions are binding across the nation. Decisions of a Court of Appeals, for instance, are binding only in the circuit over which the court has jurisdiction.
q1: Inferior courts cannot determine constitutionality. (true/false)
q2: Only Supreme Court decisions are binding across the entire nation. (true/false)

story: The power to review the constitutionality of laws may be limited by Congress, which has the power to set the jurisdiction of the courts. The only constitutional limit on Congress' power to set the jurisdiction of the judiciary relates to the Supreme Court; the Supreme Court may exercise only appellate jurisdiction except in cases involving states and cases affecting foreign ambassadors, ministers or consuls.
q1: Congress cannot limit the jurisdiction of courts. (true/false)
q2: The Supreme Court may exercise appellate jurisdiction. (true/false)

story: The Constitution does not explicitly indicate the pre-eminence of any particular branch of government. However, James Madison wrote in Federalist 51, regarding the ability of each branch to defend itself from actions by the others, that "it is not possible to give to each department an equal power of self-defense. In republican government, the legislative authority necessarily predominates."
q1: Madison wrote that the legislative branch necessarily predominates. (true/false)
q2: According to Madison, each branch has equal power of self-defense. (true/false)

story: It is said[by whom?] on one side of this debate that separation of powers means that powers are shared among different branches; no one branch may act unilaterally on issues (other than perhaps minor questions), but must obtain some form of agreement across branches. That is, it is argued that "checks and balances" apply to the Judicial branch as well as to the other branches.
q1: Under the separation of powers, one branch may act unilaterally on any issue. (true/false)
q2: Checks and balances apply to the Judicial branch. (true/false)

story: An example of the first view is the regulation of attorneys and judges, and the establishment of rules for the conduct of the courts, by the Congress and in the states the legislatures. Although in practice these matters are delegated to the Supreme Court, the Congress holds these powers and delegates them to the Supreme Court only for convenience in light of the Supreme Court's expertise, but can withdraw that delegation at any time.
q1: Congress permanently delegates authority to the Supreme Court and cannot withdraw it. (true/false)
q2: Congress delegates court rules authority to the Supreme Court for convenience. (true/false)

story: An example of the second view at the State level is found in the view of the Florida Supreme Court, that only the Florida Supreme Court may license and regulate attorneys appearing before the courts of Florida, and only the Florida Supreme Court may set rules for procedures in the Florida courts.[citation needed] The State of New Hampshire also follows this system.[citation needed]
q1: Only the Florida Supreme Court may license attorneys in Florida. (true/false)
q2: Florida is the only state that follows the system of state court authority over rules. (true/false)

story: The 19th-century English art critic, John Ruskin, in his Seven Lamps of Architecture, published 1849, was much narrower in his view of what constituted architecture. Architecture was the "art which so disposes and adorns the edifices raised by men ... that the sight of them" contributes "to his mental health, power, and pleasure".
q1: John Ruskin published Seven Lamps of Architecture in 1849. (true/false)
q2: Ruskin believed architecture should contribute to mental health and pleasure. (true/false)

story: On the difference between the ideals of architecture and mere construction, the renowned 20th-century architect Le Corbusier wrote: "You employ stone, wood, and concrete, and with these materials you build houses and palaces: that is construction. Ingenuity is at work. But suddenly you touch my heart, you do me good. I am happy and I say: This is beautiful. That is Architecture".
q1: Le Corbusier was a 19th-century architect. (true/false)
q2: Le Corbusier said that architecture touches the heart. (true/false)

story: While the notion that structural and aesthetic considerations should be entirely subject to functionality was met with both popularity and skepticism, it had the effect of introducing the concept of "function" in place of Vitruvius' "utility". "Function" came to be seen as encompassing all criteria of the use, perception and enjoyment of a building, not only practical but also aesthetic, psychological and cultural.
q1: The concept of function replaced Vitruvius' concept of utility. (true/false)
q2: Function encompasses only practical criteria. (true/false)

story: Building first evolved out of the dynamics between needs (shelter, security, worship, etc.) and means (available building materials and attendant skills). As human cultures developed and knowledge began to be formalized through oral traditions and practices, building became a craft, and "architecture" is the name given to the most highly formalized and respected versions of that craft.
q1: Architecture is the least formalized version of building craft. (true/false)
q2: Building evolved from the interaction of needs and available means. (true/false)

story: Islamic architecture began in the 7th century CE, incorporating architectural forms from the ancient Middle East and Byzantium, but also developing features to suit the religious and social needs of the society. Examples can be found throughout the Middle East, North Africa, Spain and the Indian Sub-continent. The widespread application of the pointed arch was to influence European architecture of the Medieval period.
q1: Islamic architecture incorporated forms from Byzantium. (true/false)
q2: The pointed arch originated in Islamic architecture. (true/false)
